_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against AGA Clinical Practice Update, Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18), AASLD Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis guidance, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Cholangitis: Symptoms, Types, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Cholangitis refers to inflammation and infection of the bile ducts, the tubular network that carries bile from the liver and gallbladder to the small intestine. The most common form, acute cholangitis (ascending cholangitis), is a potentially life-threatening biliary emergency caused by bacterial infection of an obstructed bile duct — classically presenting with the Charcot triad of fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant pain. Chronic cholangitis, most commonly primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), involves progressive inflammation, fibrosis, and stricturing of the bile ducts over years, culminating in biliary cirrhosis and liver failure. Prompt recognition is critical: acute cholangitis can progress to septic shock within hours without urgent biliary drainage, while PSC carries a significantly elevated risk of cholangiocarcinoma requiring lifelong surveillance.

keyStats.prevalence: Acute cholangitis accounts for 7-13% of all biliary tract admissions; PSC prevalence ranges from 4-16 per 100,000 in North America and Europe
keyStats.demographics: Acute cholangitis is slightly more common in males (M:F ratio 1.5:1); PSC shows a male predominance (M:F 2:1) with typical onset between ages 25-45
keyStats.avgAge: Acute cholangitis peak incidence is 50-70 years; PSC median age at diagnosis is 32-40 years
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 200,000-500,000 cases of acute cholangitis annually worldwide, though incidence varies significantly by region and underlying etiology

definition: Cholangitis (ICD-10: K83.0 for cholangitis, K74.5 for primary sclerosing cholangitis) is inflammation of the bile ducts caused by bacterial infection (acute cholangitis) or autoimmune/idiopathic processes (chronic cholangitis, PSC). Acute cholangitis is almost always preceded by biliary obstruction — from gallstones, strictures, malignancy, or biliary stent obstruction — which allows gut bacteria (most commonly Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, and Bacteroides) to colonize and infect the static bile. The classic Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) is present in 50-70% of cases; Reynolds' pentad adds hypotension and altered mental status, signaling severe sepsis. Chronic cholangitis from PSC involves progressive concentric fibrosis ('onion-skin' pattern) of intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts, leading to strictures, cholestasis, and ultimately biliary cirrhosis. PSC is strongly associated with inflammatory bowel disease — 70-80% of PSC patients have concurrent ulcerative colitis.

typesClassification.description: The classic biliary emergency. Bacterial infection of an obstructed bile duct, typically from a stone or stricture. Presents with Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) or Reynolds' pentad (adding shock and confusion) in severe cases. Requires urgent biliary drainage via ERCP or percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) in addition to broad-spectrum antibiotics.

A chronic progressive disorder of unknown etiology characterized by inflammation, fibrosis, and stricturing of intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts. Leads to biliary cirrhosis, portal hypertension, and liver failure. Strongly associated with ulcerative colitis (70-80% of cases) and to a lesser extent Crohn disease. There is no cure; management focuses on symptom control, complication management, and surveillance for cholangiocarcinoma.

Bile duct fibrosis and stricturing caused by identifiable secondary causes: recurrent pyogenic cholangitis (biliary parasites such as Clonorchis sinensis or Ascaris lumbricoides), ischemic cholangiopathy (post-liver transplant hepatic artery stenosis, shock), surgical bile duct injury, or autoimmune pancreatitis (IgG4-related cholangitis).

A disease complex predominantly seen in East and Southeast Asian populations, characterized by intrahepatic bile duct strictures, pigment gallstones, and recurrent bacterial cholangitis. Associated with chronic biliary parasite infestation. Often requires aggressive interventional management including PTC drainage and surgery for recurrent strictures.

Bile duct strictures and cholangitis occurring in patients with advanced HIV/AIDS (typically CD4 <100 cells/microliter) due to opportunistic infections including Cryptosporidium, Cytomegalovirus, and Microsporidia. Highly active antiretroviral therapy (HAART) has dramatically reduced incidence.

primarySymptoms: Fever with chills — the most common presenting symptom in acute cholangitis, reflecting bacteremia and systemic inflammatory response. Fever is present in 80-90% of acute cholangitis cases.
Right upper quadrant or epigastric abdominal pain — dull, constant pain radiating to the right scapula or shoulder, reflecting biliary distension. Present in 70-80% of acute cholangitis cases.

Jaundice — yellowing of skin and sclera from conjugated hyperbilirubinemia due to biliary obstruction. Visible jaundice is present in 60-80% of acute cholangitis cases but may be absent early in the disease course.
Nausea and vomiting — nonspecific but common, particularly when biliary obstruction triggers simultaneous gallbladder contraction against a blocked cystic or common bile duct.
Pruritus (itching) — from bile salt deposition in the skin, prominent in PSC and chronic cholangitis due to cholestasis. Often worsens at night and can be severe enough to impair sleep.

Pale, greasy, floating stools (acholic stools) — from fat malabsorption due to absent intestinal bile salts. A sign of complete biliary obstruction, seen in both acute cholangitis and PSC with dominant strictures.
Dark urine — from conjugated bilirubinuria, another sign of biliary obstruction. Urine turns the color of cola or dark beer.
Malaise and anorexia — generalized symptoms of systemic infection in acute cholangitis, or chronic cholestasis in PSC.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset jaundice with fever — the hallmark combination that should trigger immediate evaluation for biliary obstruction and cholangitis
Fever of unknown origin with RUQ discomfort — cholangitis can present with fever preceding overt jaundice by 24-48 hours
Pruritus without rash in a patient with known IBD — PSC is frequently diagnosed this way, and PSC-associated cholangitis carries malignancy risk
Recurrent episodes of fever and jaundice following previous biliary surgery or ERCP — suggests benign stricturing with recurrent pyogenic cholangitis
Progressive fatigue and weight loss in a patient with known PSC — warrants urgent evaluation for cholangiocarcinoma
emergencySigns: Reynolds' pentad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain, hypotension, altered mental status) — indicates septic shock from acute cholangitis and requires immediate resuscitation and urgent biliary decompression
Septic shock: tachycardia >120 bpm, systolic BP <90 mmHg, lactate >2 mmol/L — high mortality (up to 25-30%) without immediate intervention
Altered mental status in the setting of fever and jaundice — sepsis-associated encephalopathy or direct effect of biliary toxins
Severe worsening abdominal pain with peritoneal signs — possible biliary perforation or progression to suppurative cholangitis
Rapidly progressive jaundice with coagulopathy (INR >1.5) and low albumin — liver synthetic failure in end-stage PSC

causes.description: The most common cause of acute cholangitis. A stone lodged in the common bile duct (choledocholithiasis) obstructs bile flow, creating static bile in which bacteria proliferate. The combination of obstruction + infection + bacteria defines ascending cholangitis. Approximately 10-15% of patients with gallstones develop common bile duct stones, and a subset of these progress to cholangitis.

Benign strictures (post-surgical, chronic pancreatitis-induced, or primary sclerosing cholangitis) or malignant strictures (pancreatic head cancer, cholangiocarcinoma, gallbladder cancer) obstruct bile flow and predispose to cholangitis. Malignant strictures are a particularly common precipitant in older adults presenting with cholangitis.

Indwelling biliary stents become colonized with bacteria and can obstruct, precipitating cholangitis. Stent occlusion rates increase with time — plastic stents occlude within 3-6 months; even metal stents eventually occlude from sludge or tumor ingrowth.

The gut microbiome is the source of biliary bacteria. Organisms that normally reside in the colon (E. coli, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Enterococcus species, Bacteroides fragilis, and anaerobes) ascend the bile duct via the sphincter of Oddi. Bile is normally sterile; loss of the bacteriostatic properties of bile (via obstruction) allows bacterial overgrowth.

Primary sclerosing cholangitis is an immune-mediated disease with strong HLA associations (HLA-B8, HLA-DR3, HLA-DRB1), associated with elevated IgG4 in the related condition IgG4-related cholangitis. Both involve T-cell mediated inflammation of bile duct epithelium leading to progressive fibrosis and stricturing.

Chronic biliary parasites — Clonorchis sinensis (Chinese liver fluke, endemic in East Asia), Opisthorchis viverrini, and Ascaris lumbricoides — cause direct epithelial injury, bile duct strictures, and recurrent pyogenic cholangitis. Associated with consumption of raw or undercooked freshwater fish and contaminated produce.

Bile duct epithelium is dependent on hepatic arterial blood supply; ischemia from hepatic artery thrombosis, shock, or vasculitis causes bile duct necrosis and stricturing. Seen in liver transplant recipients with hepatic artery stenosis, patients with severe hypotension, and those with polyarteritis nodosa.

riskFactors.description: The single most important risk factor for acute cholangitis. Prior cholecystectomy reduces but does not eliminate risk, as stones can form in the bile ducts themselves.

Prior biliary surgery and ERCP with sphincterotomy create a permanently dilated, incompetent sphincter of Oddi, facilitating bacterial reflux into the bile ducts and predisposing to recurrent cholangitis.
Approximately 70-80% of PSC patients have concurrent ulcerative colitis; 5-7% of UC patients develop PSC. IBD is a major risk factor for PSC.
Pancreatic inflammation and fibrosis can compress the distal common bile duct, causing obstructive cholestasis and cholangitis. Alcohol-related chronic pancreatitis is the most common cause.
Patients with indwelling plastic biliary stents should undergo stent exchange every 3-6 months to prevent occlusion and cholangitis.
Acute cholangitis incidence and mortality increase significantly with age due to reduced physiological reserve, comorbidities, and delayed presentation.
Diabetes impairs immune responses to bacterial infection and is associated with increased severity and mortality in acute cholangitis.
Endemic to Southeast Asia, China, Korea, and parts of Eastern Europe. Chronic fluke infection causes recurrent pyogenic cholangitis and carries a 5-15 fold increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma.
AIDS cholangiopathy and opportunistic biliary infections occur at CD4 counts <100. Transplant recipients on immunosuppression are at increased risk for biliary infections from atypical organisms.
Acute cholangitis is 1.5x more common in males, partly reflecting higher rates of gallstone disease and alcohol-related biliary pathology in men.

affectedDemographics: Acute cholangitis is most common in adults aged 50-70, reflecting the age distribution of gallstone disease and biliary pathology
PSC is predominantly a disease of young adults: median diagnosis age 32-40 years, with a second smaller peak in childhood
PSC shows a 2:1 male-to-female predominance, in contrast to ulcerative colitis which is slightly more common in females
Recurrent pyogenic cholangitis and parasitic cholangitis are predominantly seen in people from or with travel history to endemic regions
AIDS-related cholangiopathy has become rare in the post-HAART era but still occurs in late-presenting HIV patients
PSC carries a markedly elevated risk of cholangiocarcinoma (lifetime risk 10-20%) and colorectal cancer
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of acute cholangitis is based on the Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18), which establish a three-tier severity grading system and criteria for diagnosis. The TG18 diagnostic criteria require: Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) OR two of the triad plus laboratory evidence of inflammation (elevated WBC, CRP) AND evidence of biliary obstruction (dilated ducts on imaging or elevated ALP/bilirubin). For PSC, diagnosis requires characteristic cholestatic liver blood tests (elevated ALP, GGT), imaging showing multifocal bile duct strictures ('beaded' appearance), and exclusion of secondary causes. MR cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) is the preferred non-invasive imaging to visualize bile duct strictures. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) is both diagnostic and therapeutic — allowing direct cholangiography, brushings for cytology, and biliary drainage.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard non-invasive imaging for biliary disease. MRCP visualizes the entire biliary tree, identifies the level and cause of obstruction, characterizes stricture morphology, and assesses for PSC multifocal stricturing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute MRI scan of the abdomen with no contrast or gadolinium-based contrast for certain indications. Non-invasive, no radiation, highly accurate (>95% sensitivity for biliary obstruction).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Both diagnostic and therapeutic. Allows direct visualization and contrast injection into the bile ducts, stone extraction, stricture dilation, stent placement, and brushings for cytology/histology. The primary therapeutic intervention for acute cholangitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An endoscopic procedure under sedation or general anesthesia. A duodenoscope is passed through the mouth to the duodenum; the bile duct opening is cannulated. Therapeutic interventions are performed during the same procedure. Risks include pancreatitis (2-5%), bleeding (after sphincterotomy), and perforation (<1%).

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests and bilirubin panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Cholestatic pattern: elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP), gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), and conjugated (direct) bilirubin indicates biliary obstruction. In PSC, ALP is typically 2-10x upper limit of normal. In acute cholangitis, WBC is elevated with a left shift.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Results available within hours. ALP elevation alone is nonspecific; the combination of elevated ALP + GGT + direct bilirubin is highly suggestive of biliary pathology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to evaluate biliary dilation (indicating obstruction), gallstones, gallbladder wall thickness, and liver parenchyma. In acute cholangitis, ultrasound may show dilated intrahepatic bile ducts.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute non-invasive scan using a transducer on the abdomen. No radiation, no contrast. Patient should fast for 6-8 hours prior for optimal gallbladder visualization.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Useful when ultrasound is inconclusive and for evaluating potential causes of biliary obstruction. Less sensitive than MRCP for visualizing the biliary tree itself.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 15-20 minute CT scan with intravenous contrast. Requires an IV line for contrast injection. Moderate radiation exposure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Blood cultures are positive in 50-70% of acute cholangitis cases and guide antibiotic de-escalation. Bile obtained during ERCP can be sent for culture and sensitivity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets of blood cultures from separate venipuncture sites before antibiotics are initiated. Bile culture is obtained during ERCP if performed. Results in 2-5 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of acute cholangitis follows the Tokyo Guidelines framework: hemodynamic resuscitation, broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics, and urgent biliary drainage (ERCP or PTC) for moderate-to-severe cases. The timing of biliary drainage is critical — within 24 hours for moderate disease, within 12 hours for severe disease with organ dysfunction. Antibiotics alone are insufficient when obstruction is present; drainage is mandatory. ERCP with endoscopic sphincterotomy, stone extraction, and stent or nasobiliary drainage is the preferred approach. For PSC, there is no cure — management focuses on treating dominant strictures endoscopically (ERCP with balloon dilation), managing pruritus (cholestyramine, rifampicin, naltrexone), supplementing fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), and treating metabolic bone disease. Liver transplantation is the only curative option for end-stage PSC.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics — Piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftriaxone + metronidazole, or carbapenems
medicalTreatments.description: Empiric antibiotics must cover gram-negative enteric bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella), gram-positive enterococci, and anaerobes. Piperacillin-tazobactam is a common first-line choice. Antibiotics are continued for 4-7 days after successful biliary drainage and clinical resolution.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotics alone are insufficient for biliary obstruction; they are adjunctive to drainage. Appropriate antibiotics reduce mortality from 30% to <10% in severe cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Urgent biliary drainage via ERCP with sphincterotomy, stone extraction, and nasobiliary stent
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment for acute cholangitis with obstruction. Endoscopic sphincterotomy allows stone extraction; a nasobiliary drain or plastic stent maintains biliary drainage. ERCP should be performed within 24 hours of presentation for moderate cases, within 12 hours for severe cases with organ dysfunction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ERCP achieves biliary drainage in >90% of cases. Mortality increases significantly with each hour delay in biliary decompression in severe cholangitis.

medicalTreatments.name: Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) with external drainage
medicalTreatments.description: A catheter is inserted through the liver parenchyma into a dilated intrahepatic bile duct under ultrasound/fluoroscopic guidance, providing external biliary drainage. Used when ERCP is not feasible (altered anatomy) or has failed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: External biliary drainage is achieved in >90% of PTC attempts. Effective at relieving sepsis but requires the catheter to remain in place until definitive intervention can be performed.

medicalTreatments.name: Endoscopic balloon dilation of PSC dominant strictures
medicalTreatments.description: ERCP with balloon dilation (4-6 mm balloon) of dominant strictures in PSC to relieve cholestasis and prevent recurrent cholangitis. Dilation is typically repeated every 6-12 months as needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Dilation provides symptomatic and biochemical improvement in 70-80% of dominant strictures. Repeated dilation is preferred over indefinite stenting in PSC.

medicalTreatments.name: Cholestyramine 4-16 g/day in divided doses (for pruritus in chronic cholangitis/PSC)
medicalTreatments.description: A bile acid-binding resin that interrupts the enterohepatic circulation of bile acids, reducing circulating bile salt levels and pruritus. First-line for cholestatic itch.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in 60-80% of patients with cholestatic pruritus. GI side effects (constipation, bloating, nausea) limit tolerability.

medicalTreatments.name: Rifampicin 150-600 mg/day (adjunctive for cholestatic pruritus refractory to cholestyramine)
medicalTreatments.description: A bacterial antibiotic with potent anti-pruritic effects in cholestasis. Started at low dose and titrated up. Mechanism may involve P450 enzyme induction, altered bile acid metabolism, or central opioid antagonism.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pruritus scores by 40-60% in rifampicin-responsive patients within 1-2 weeks. Hepatotoxicity is a concern — liver function tests must be monitored.

surgicalOptions.name: Cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration (CBDE)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the gallbladder with concurrent exploration and clearance of the common bile duct for stones or strictures. Indicated when ERCP cannot clear biliary obstruction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: CBDE achieves stone clearance in 85-95% of cases when performed by experienced surgeons. Laparoscopic CBDE is standard in modern practice.

surgicalOptions.name: Hepatic lobectomy for recurrent pyogenic cholangitis with intrahepatic stones
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical resection of affected liver segments containing intrahepatic stones and strictures in recurrent pyogenic cholangitis. Combined with extrahepatic biliary surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Effective at eliminating the source of intrahepatic stone formation in 80-90% of selected patients. Morbidity from major hepatectomy is significant (20-30%).

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation for end-stage PSC
surgicalOptions.description: The only curative treatment for PSC with liver failure (decompensated cirrhosis, MELD >15), refractory cholangitis episodes, or PSC with unresectable cholangiocarcinoma (within Milan criteria).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Excellent outcomes: 1-year patient survival 85-90%, 10-year survival 65-70%. PSC recurs in 20-30% of transplant recipients at 5-10 years but is usually not clinically significant.

surgicalOptions.name: Biliary bypass surgery (Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy)
surgicalOptions.description: A surgical bypass connecting the bile duct above a benign stricture to the jejunum, creating a permanent drainage pathway. Indicated for benign strictures that recur after multiple endoscopic dilations.
surgicalOptions.successRate: High long-term patency (80-90% at 5 years) for properly selected benign strictures. Requires major abdominal surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 15-30 mg/kg/day
alternativeTreatments.description: A hydrophilic bile acid that is the standard medical therapy for PSC. UDCA improves liver biochemistries and may slow disease progression. Standard-dose UDCA (15-20 mg/kg) improves ALP and liver enzymes in 50-60% of PSC patients.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Widely used as a standard of care despite limited hard endpoint data. Does not reverse fibrosis and its impact on survival is unproven.

alternativeTreatments.name: Endoscopic ultrasound-guided biliary drainage (EUS-BD)
alternativeTreatments.description: An advanced interventional endoscopy technique where EUS is used to access the bile duct transgastrically or transduodenally and place a stent or drain. Used when ERCP fails due to tumor obstruction or altered anatomy.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Technical success rates of 80-90% in expert centers. Comparable clinical outcomes to PTC as a second-line intervention after failed ERCP.

alternativeTreatments.name: Immunosuppressive therapy for PSC (investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: Multiple immunosuppressants have been studied in PSC: corticosteroids, azathioprine, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and anti-TNF agents — none have demonstrated convincing efficacy in large trials.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: At present, no immunosuppressant is standard of care for PSC outside of clinical trials. This remains an area of active investigation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supportive and nutritional management
alternativeTreatments.description: Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K) is essential in chronic cholangitis/PSC with steatorrhea. Medium-chain triglyceride (MCT) oil supplements aid fat absorption. Bone density scanning (DEXA) for osteoporosis screening every 2-3 years.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Vitamin supplementation corrects deficiencies and prevents complications. Nutritional support improves quality of life and functional status.

whySeeSpecialist: Cholangitis is a medical emergency that requires hospitalization, IV antibiotics, and urgent biliary drainage — typically coordinated between gastroenterology (for ERCP), hepatology (for PSC management), and interventional radiology (for PTC). A gastroenterologist or hepatologist should be involved in all cases of acute cholangitis and all cases of PSC. Referral is urgent when Charcot triad or Reynolds' pentad is present.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute cholangitis, seek care at a hospital with 24/7 interventional endoscopy capability (ERCP) and interventional radiology — ideally a tertiary center with hepatobiliary surgery available. For PSC, see a hepatologist at a transplant center with a dedicated PSC/multidisciplinary biliary disease clinic. Ask whether the hepatologist participates in PSC clinical trials.
preventionStrategies: Undergo cholecystectomy promptly if gallstones are discovered and symptomatic — delays increase the risk of biliary complications including cholangitis
If you have an indwelling biliary stent, adhere to scheduled stent exchange intervals — stent occlusion is a preventable cause of cholangitis
Manage chronic pancreatitis and its complications actively with your gastroenterologist to prevent distal CBD strictures
If you have known PSC with dominant strictures, maintain regular follow-up with ERCP as indicated — proactive stricture dilation reduces cholangitis episodes
Avoid consumption of raw or undercooked freshwater fish if living in or traveling from liver fluke endemic regions
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a healthy weight — obesity and metabolic syndrome increase gallstone risk, the leading cause of acute cholangitis
Follow a low-fat diet during episodes of biliary symptoms — fat stimulates cholecystokinin release, contracting the gallbladder against an obstruction
Stay well-hydrated — dehydration concentrates bile and may promote biliary stasis and stone formation
If you have PSC with steatorrhea, use MCT oil for cooking and supplement with fat-soluble vitamins as prescribed
Avoid alcohol entirely if you have PSC — alcohol accelerates progression to cirrhosis in any cholestatic liver disease

recommended: Low-fat diet (30-40 g fat/day or less during active symptoms) — reduces gallbladder contraction and biliary pain
Small, frequent meals — avoids prolonged fasting and large meals
High-fiber diet (25-35 g/day) — fiber binds bile acids and may modestly reduce cholesterol saturation of bile
Medium-chain triglycerides (MCT oil) — directly absorbed without bile salts, used as a fat source in PSC with steatorrhea
Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (A, D, E, K) — prescribed by a hepatologist for chronic cholestasis
Clear liquids and bland diet during acute cholangitis hospitalization
avoid: High-fat and fried foods — stimulate strong gallbladder contraction that worsens pain when obstruction is present
Processed foods and refined carbohydrates — contribute to obesity and metabolic syndrome
Alcohol — hepatotoxic and contraindicated in PSC and any form of chronic liver disease
Raw or undercooked freshwater fish and shellfish — risk of parasitic biliary infection in endemic regions
Raw eggs and undercooked poultry — risk of Salmonella which can colonize the biliary tree in immunocompromised patients

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is beneficial for general hepatobiliary health and metabolic risk reduction, but must be tailored to the clinical situation. During acute cholangitis hospitalization, activity is limited to gentle ambulation. After recovery, moderate aerobic exercise supports weight management. In PSC with cirrhosis, avoid heavy lifting if portal hypertension with splenomegaly or thrombocytopenia is present.
dailyManagement: If you have an indwelling biliary stent, maintain a calendar of scheduled exchange dates — missed exchanges are a preventable cause of cholangitis
Monitor stool and urine color — persistent pale stools or dark urine after a biliary procedure may indicate new obstruction
Take UDCA as prescribed if you have PSC — it must be taken consistently for biochemical benefit
Report fever or new RUQ pain promptly to your hepatologist — recurrent cholangitis is a sign of a dominant stricture requiring ERCP evaluation
Take fat-soluble vitamins with a fat-containing meal to ensure absorption
prognosis: Acute cholangitis carries a mortality of 5-10% with prompt drainage and appropriate antibiotics, rising to 25-40% if septic shock develops before drainage is achieved. Delay in biliary decompression is the single most important modifiable predictor of mortality. For PSC, the natural history is one of progressive cholestasis over 10-20 years from diagnosis to liver failure — median transplant-free survival is 12-15 years. Cholangiocarcinoma develops in 10-20% of PSC patients and significantly worsens prognosis.
recoveryTimeline: Acute cholangitis: hospitalization typically lasts 5-10 days. IV antibiotics are continued for 4-7 days after clinical resolution. ERCP with stone extraction or stent placement provides immediate drainage; symptoms typically improve within 24-48 hours. Full recovery to baseline takes 2-4 weeks. PSC: there is no cure. Patients require lifelong surveillance. Liver transplant evaluation is initiated when MELD-Na score reaches 15 or when complications supervene. Post-transplant survival is excellent.
complications: Septic shock and multi-organ failure — the most feared complication of acute cholangitis; mortality rises to 30-50% in frank septic shock despite resuscitation
Biliary abscess — a loculated collection of pus within the liver parenchyma, typically in an obstructed intrahepatic duct. Requires percutaneous drainage and prolonged antibiotics
Biliary strictures and recurrent cholangitis — scar tissue from inflammation creates strictures that predispose to future cholangitis episodes
Secondary biliary cirrhosis — progressive fibrosis and loss of bile ducts from chronic cholangitis, culminating in cirrhosis and portal hypertension
Choledocholithiasis (retained or recurrent bile duct stones) — after initial ERCP for cholangitis, recurrent stones form in 10-15% of patients
Cholangitis recurrence — 20-30% of patients with gallstone-related cholangitis experience recurrent cholangitis within 2 years
Cholangiocarcinoma — in PSC, chronic bile duct inflammation drives dysplasia and malignancy
Portal hypertension complications in PSC — as cirrhosis develops, portal hypertension produces splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, esophageal varices, ascites, and hepatic encephalopathy

supportResources.name: American Liver Foundation — PSC Information
supportResources.description: Patient education resources, support groups, and navigation assistance for PSC patients.

supportResources.name: PSC Partners Seeking a Cure
supportResources.description: A nonprofit dedicated to providing education and support for PSC patients and their families, funding PSC research, and connecting patients with clinical trials.

supportResources.name: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Acute Cholangitis. American Gastroenterological Association; 2023.
supportResources.description: Expert clinical guidance on diagnosis, severity grading, and management of acute cholangitis from the AGA.

supportResources.name: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18) for Acute Cholangitis
supportResources.description: The international consensus guideline on severity grading and management of acute cholangitis, including diagnostic criteria and timing of biliary drainage.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Cholecystitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder itself, typically from a cystic duct stone, presenting with RUQ pain, fever, and Murphy's sign. It does not cause jaundice unless the stone also obstructs the common bile duct. Cholangitis involves infection of the bile ducts and produces jaundice by definition.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gallstones (Cholelithiasis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gallstones in the gallbladder cause biliary colic and can cause cholecystitis, but do not typically cause jaundice or cholangitis unless a stone migrates into the common bile duct.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Pancreatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute pancreatitis presents with epigastric pain radiating to the back, elevated lipase and amylase, and does not typically cause jaundice. Cholangitis and gallstone pancreatitis can both be caused by a common bile duct stone and may coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PBC is an autoimmune disease primarily affecting the small intrahepatic bile ducts (interlobular ducts), presenting with isolated cholestasis and anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA). PSC affects larger intra- and extrahepatic ducts, produces multifocal strictures ('beaded' appearance), is male-predominant, and is associated with IBD.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Cholecystitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute inflammation of the gallbladder, often coexists with cholangitis when gallstones obstruct both the cystic and common bile ducts. Both are biliary emergencies requiring prompt intervention.

relatedConditions.name: Primary Biliary Cholangitis (PBC)
relatedConditions.relevance: An autoimmune liver disease that, like PSC, causes chronic bile duct inflammation and cholestasis. PBC affects small interlobular bile ducts; PSC affects larger ducts.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Pancreatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic pancreatic inflammation can obstruct the distal common bile duct and predispose to cholangitis. Both conditions may coexist in alcohol-related disease.

relatedConditions.name: Choledocholithiasis (Common Bile Duct Stones)
relatedConditions.relevance: Stones in the common bile duct are the most common cause of acute cholangitis. Choledocholithiasis without infection is managed electively; with infection it becomes a biliary emergency.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cholangitis and cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: Cholangitis is infection of the bile ducts (the tubes carrying bile from the liver to the intestine), almost always caused by obstruction. Cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder itself, usually from a stone blocking the cystic duct. The key distinguishing feature is jaundice — cholangitis almost always causes jaundice from bile duct obstruction; cholecystitis typically does not unless it simultaneously involves the common bile duct.

faqs.question: What is primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC)?
faqs.answer: PSC is a chronic progressive liver disease of unknown cause characterized by inflammation and fibrosis of the bile ducts, leading to strictures, cholestasis, and eventually cirrhosis. It is strongly associated with inflammatory bowel disease — 70-80% of PSC patients have ulcerative colitis. There is no cure; management focuses on treating complications and preparing for liver transplant when liver failure develops.

faqs.question: What is the Charcot triad?
faqs.answer: Charcot triad is the classic combination of fever, jaundice, and right upper quadrant abdominal pain — the hallmark presentation of acute ascending cholangitis. It is present in 50-70% of acute cholangitis cases. Reynolds' pentad adds hypotension and altered mental status, indicating septic shock and severe disease requiring immediate intervention.

faqs.question: How is acute cholangitis treated?
faqs.answer: Acute cholangitis is treated with a three-pronged approach: fluid resuscitation and hemodynamic support, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering gram-negative, gram-positive, and anaerobic bacteria, and urgent biliary drainage to relieve the obstruction. Biliary drainage is most commonly performed via ERCP (endoscopic stent placement or stone extraction). Without drainage, antibiotics alone are insufficient and mortality approaches 100%.

faqs.question: Is cholangitis life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Yes — acute cholangitis is a potentially fatal biliary emergency. Mortality with prompt treatment (antibiotics + drainage within 24 hours) is 5-10%. Mortality rises to 25-40% if septic shock develops before drainage is achieved. Delay in biliary decompression is the single most important modifiable predictor of death.

faqs.question: What causes cholangitis?
faqs.answer: Acute cholangitis is caused by bacterial infection of an obstructed bile duct. The obstruction is most commonly a gallstone in the common bile duct, followed by benign or malignant strictures, biliary stents, and biliary parasites. Bacteria from the gut (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Bacteroides) colonize the static bile. Chronic cholangitis (PSC) is caused by autoimmune inflammation of the bile ducts.

faqs.question: What is ERCP and why is it needed for cholangitis?
faqs.answer: ERCP (endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography) is a procedure where an endoscope is passed through the mouth to the duodenum, the bile duct opening is cut (sphincterotomy), and stones are extracted or a stent is placed to drain infected bile. ERCP is both diagnostic (visualizing the bile ducts) and therapeutic (relieving the obstruction). It is the primary treatment for acute cholangitis with biliary obstruction.

faqs.question: Can cholangitis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes — recurrent cholangitis is common, particularly in patients who do not have their gallbladder removed after a gallstone-related episode (10-15% recurrence rate within 2 years), patients with indwelling biliary stents that are not exchanged regularly, and PSC patients with dominant strictures. Ongoing surveillance and management of underlying risk factors reduces recurrence risk.

faqs.question: How is PSC monitored over time?
faqs.answer: PSC patients require lifelong monitoring: liver function tests and ALP every 3-6 months, MRCP every 1-2 years (or when symptoms change) to assess stricture progression, annual colonoscopy (in those with IBD) for colorectal cancer surveillance, and DEXA scan every 2-3 years for osteoporosis.

faqs.question: Is PSC inherited?
faqs.answer: PSC has a genetic component — there is a 2-5% prevalence in first-degree relatives of PSC patients (compared to 0.02% in the general population), and multiple HLA gene associations are established. However, most PSC cases are sporadic, and the risk to children of PSC patients is low.

faqs.question: What is the connection between PSC and ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: This is one of the strongest disease associations in medicine: 70-80% of PSC patients have concurrent inflammatory bowel disease, predominantly ulcerative colitis. Conversely, 5-7% of UC patients will develop PSC. The two conditions likely share immune-mediated pathways. All PSC patients should have a colonoscopy at diagnosis; all UC patients with abnormal liver tests should be evaluated for PSC.

metaTitle: Cholangitis: Types, Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Cholangitis: acute bile duct infection and chronic PSC. Learn about symptoms, causes, Charcot triad, ERCP treatment, and PSC management. Reviewed by our medical team.

images.altText: Anatomy of the biliary system showing the liver, gallbladder, and bile ducts where cholangitis occurs
images.caption: The biliary system: cholangitis involves infection and inflammation of the bile ducts, most commonly the common bile duct and intrahepatic ducts.

images.altText: Illustration of the liver and gallbladder showing the bile ducts, hepatic duct, cystic duct, and common bile duct
images.caption: The liver produces bile, which is stored in the gallbladder and transported to the intestine via the bile ducts — the structures affected by cholangitis.

images.altText: MRCP image showing dilated bile ducts in a patient with biliary obstruction and cholangitis
images.caption: MRCP (magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography) is the gold-standard non-invasive imaging for visualizing bile duct strictures and obstruction in cholangitis and PSC.
